description: UE5 IK: foot placement, Two Bone IK, Full Body IK, CCDIK, and Motion Warping for root motion alignment

entries.title: Inverse Kinematics (IK) Overview
entries.description: IK adjusts bone positions to reach target points — feet planted on uneven ground, hands gripping objects, heads looking at targets. UE5 provides IK solvers as AnimGraph nodes.
entries.whatItIs: Inverse Kinematics (IK) is a technique that calculates bone rotations to make an end effector (hand, foot) reach a target position. Unlike forward kinematics (playing authored animations), IK dynamically adapts the skeleton to the environment — planting feet on stairs, reaching for ledges, or aiming weapons at targets.
whenToUse: Foot IK — planting feet on uneven terrain, stairs, and slopes so characters don't float or clip
Hand IK — gripping ledges, holding weapons, interacting with objects at varying heights
Look At — head and upper body turning to face a target (dialogue, aiming)
Aiming — adjusting the spine and arms to point a weapon at a dynamic target
Placing hands on walls when leaning or for touch feedback
whenNotToUse: When the animation already handles placement perfectly — authored animations for flat ground don't need foot IK
For full-body procedural animation with no authored base — IK works best as a layer on top of existing animations
For simple characters where slight floating doesn't matter (stylized, 2D, far-away NPCs)
commonMistakes: Not using traces to find the ground position for foot IK — the IK target must come from a line trace, not a guess
Setting IK blend weight to 1.0 instantly instead of interpolating — causes snapping instead of smooth adjustment
Applying IK before the base animation in the AnimGraph — IK should be applied after the base pose as a post-process
Not adjusting the pelvis/hips when feet are at different heights — one leg stretches while the other clips
Using Two Bone IK for chains with more than 2 bones — use CCDIK or FABRIK for longer chains
keyPoints: Two Bone IK: solves a 2-bone chain (upper arm + forearm, thigh + shin). Most common for hands and feet.
FABRIK (Forward And Backward Reaching IK): iterative solver for chains of any length. Good for tails, tentacles.
CCDIK (Cyclic Coordinate Descent): another multi-bone solver. Good for spines and long chains.
Full Body IK: solves the entire skeleton at once. More expensive but produces natural full-body results.
Look At: rotates a bone (head, spine) to face a target world location.
All IK nodes are AnimGraph nodes — add them after your base animation pose.
Effector Location: the target position the IK tries to reach (from a trace or gameplay logic).
Alpha (Blend Weight): 0=no IK, 1=full IK. Interpolate for smooth activation.

entries.title: Motion Warping
entries.description: Motion Warping modifies root motion animations at runtime to align the character with dynamic targets — lunging to an enemy's exact position, landing on a ledge, or syncing with moving objects.
entries.whatItIs: UMotionWarpingComponent adjusts the root motion of an animation in real-time so that the character reaches a specific target location and/or rotation by the end of the animation. This enables authored animations to work with dynamic gameplay targets (enemies at varying distances, ledges at different heights).
whenToUse: Melee attacks that need to close the gap to the target — lunge forward to wherever the enemy is
Ledge grabs and vaulting — the character needs to land exactly on the ledge regardless of height
Assassination/finisher animations — attacker needs to reach the exact position relative to the victim
Any root motion animation where the destination varies at runtime
whenNotToUse: Animations without root motion — motion warping only adjusts root motion, not in-place animations
When the character is already at the correct position — no warping needed
For precise physics-based movement — use AddImpulse or CharacterMovement instead
Simple translation that doesn't need an authored animation — just use movement interpolation
commonMistakes: Not adding a MotionWarping AnimNotifyState to the montage — the component doesn't know which frames to warp
Setting the warp target after the montage starts playing — set it before or in the same frame as Play
Using motion warping on animations without root motion — it has nothing to warp
Not matching the WarpTargetName in the notify state with the name used in AddOrUpdateWarpTarget()
Warping over too long a distance — the character slides unrealistically. Keep warp distances reasonable relative to the animation.
keyPoints: UMotionWarpingComponent: add to your character. Handles warp target registration and root motion modification.
AddOrUpdateWarpTarget(FMotionWarpingTarget): set the target location/rotation by name.
AnimNotifyState_MotionWarping: add to the montage timeline to define which frames are warped and the target name.
Root motion required: the animation must have root motion for warping to adjust anything.
Warp types: Translation (move to target), Rotation (face target), or both.
Multiple targets: different montage sections can warp to different named targets.
Remove target: RemoveWarpTarget(Name) when no longer needed.
